metrics.narrative: The buyer quantified volume but not yet dollar value. On the 5 Jun demo, Marc Quessenberry restated the agreed scope and Metallus confirmed 'approximately 90 to 100,000 invoices a year' as the processing volume, and the goal to 'reduce errors, eliminate manual receipt entries, and improve monthly accuracy and financial visibility'. On the 22 May discovery, Nicholas Galbincea described the current state as 'a manual and error-prone process, necessitating automation for the PO-based demand that integrates with Maximo, SAP, and Onbase workflows for efficient three-way matching and exception handling'. The qualitative pain and the volume are clear, but no hard cost, headcount, or savings figure has been put on the table. Quantifying the cost of the manual process (error rework, FTE hours, late-payment exposure) is the move to harden the business case.

champion.narrative: Nicholas Galbincea is the working champion: he opened the discovery, drove scope, set the original decision target, is the named point of contact on every proposal touchpoint (22 May, 5 Jun, 16 Jun, 19 Jun), and proactively emailed in on 29 Jun. He has shown advocacy by keeping the cadence tight and was 'willing to delay decision-making for a few weeks to ensure they made the best choice' (22 May), which buys us time. The limitation is seniority and breadth: Nicholas is a Manager, the deal is single-threaded around him at the decision layer, and there is no evidence he has access to or has sold internally to the budget owner. 'Developing' because the relationship is real and active but we have no second senior advocate and no confirmation he can carry the shortlist decision. The next step is to lean on his live 29 Jun thread to get the EB intro and broaden to Stephen Murphy (Director).

competition.narrative: This is a contested RFP with multiple bidders but the field is unnamed. The buyer confirmed an 'RFP process underway' with a vendor shortlist (22 May), reported our 'pricing is in line with other vendors' (19 Jun), and on 29 Jun confirmed they are 'reviewing all of our bidders' proposals' — multiple competitors confirmed, none named. The incumbent is the manual status quo (Maximo + OnBase + SAP); Metallus 'were not considering moving away from Maximo' but were open to it in future (22 May), so we must integrate with Maximo rather than displace it. We win by being the AI-driven, unified three-way-match and exception engine that natively integrates with Maximo/SAP/OnBase and folds PR approvals into the Merlin intake tool and Teams — eInvoicing is table-stakes; Merlin is the wedge. The competitive intel gap (who else is shortlisted) is the risk to close before the finalist pitch, and Nicholas's 29 Jun reply is the opening to ask.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific and owner-attributed. Nicholas Galbincea described the current invoice processing through Maximo as 'a manual and error-prone process' requiring automation that integrates with Maximo, SAP and OnBase for three-way matching and exception handling (22 May). On 5 Jun the buyer confirmed the goals to 'reduce errors, eliminate manual receipt entries, and improve monthly accuracy and financial visibility' across ~90-100k invoices a year, and to 'centralize both physical good receipts and service invoice processing in a unified solution to eliminate dual entry'. The pain owners are the AR/AP function (Robert Rummer) and the procurement/commodity team (Nicholas, Stephen). Confirmed, not inferred.

paper_process.narrative: Some contracting mechanics are confirmed. An NDA is in motion: the 22 May next step was 'Zycus to review and sign NDA, then receive sample data for integration scoping'. A Professional Services scoping session is to be scheduled to define implementation scope and timeline (Next Step, 5 Jun), and we owe a firm PS cost after that exercise (committed 29 Jun, due ~15 Jul). Beyond the NDA and PS scoping there is no visibility into MSA/SOW path, legal review, procurement channel, or InfoSec requirements, and no contract-expiration forcing function (net-new automation, not a renewal). Confirming the buyer's contracting and security steps once the shortlist is announced would create that visibility.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is mapped. We are engaged at manager and director level: Nicholas Galbincea (Manager, Commodity & Supply Chain) and Stephen Murphy (Director, General Procurement) self-introduced on the 22 May call, with Robert Rummer (Sr. Manager, AR/AP & Credit) and Valerie Johnson attending. Pricing has been discussed and was 'in line with other vendors' (19 Jun), and on 29 Jun Nicholas himself asked the pricing-clarification question — but there is still no evidence of who owns the budget or signs the ~$293k year-one envelope, and no VP/C-level contact has appeared on any call. With the shortlist likely escalating to senior stakeholders we have not met, identifying and reaching the budget owner is the single biggest open structural risk. Because this is a champion-authored RFP, the right path is to arm Nicholas to broker the introduction — use his 29 Jun reply to ask him directly who signs off and request the connect.

decision_process.narrative: The process is a formal RFP. On the 22 May discovery the buyer summarised it as 'RFP process underway; target vendor shortlist by end of May/first week of June with NDA execution, sample data exchange, deep-dive demos, and budgetary proposals as next steps'. That sequence has played out: discovery 22 May, demo 5 Jun, proposal 19 Jun. Per Next Step (19 Jun) the buyer will 'evaluate vendor proposals over the next 2 weeks' and 'announce shortlisted vendors by July 4th' (~4 days out), and the 29 Jun reply confirms they are actively 'reviewing all bidders' proposals'. The call attendance is the process map: Nicholas, Stephen, Robert and Valerie form the evaluation group, but the approval chain above them is not yet visible — the gap to close before the next gate.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well evidenced from the discovery and demo. Metallus needs PO-based invoice automation that integrates with Maximo, SAP and OnBase, performs three-way matching with an AI-driven exception queue, and handles both physical-goods receipts and service invoices in one unified flow to eliminate dual entry (Nicholas Galbincea and Marc Quessenberry, 22 May and 5 Jun). On 5 Jun the buyer pressed on whether the system reads the automated workflow from Maximo data and asked the team to 'ensure PR approval notifications are sent via email and integrated with the Merlin intake tool and Teams'. A specific requirement to 'interface with OnBase (or replace OnBase) to handle invoice approval workflows and route invoices for cost-boss approval before posting to Maximo' was captured on 22 May. Pricing being 'in line with other vendors' shows commercial fit is also a live criterion.

north_star_verdict.headline: Live, champion-driven RFP into a 4 Jul shortlist gate: positive proposal feedback, competitive pricing, and a fresh 29 Jun buyer reply that opens the door to reach power and the competitive field. Close date 6 Aug 2027 is a placeholder, not a near-term risk.

flags: Reach power through the champion before 4 Jul: no budget owner is mapped for the ~$293k year-one envelope and the decision likely escalates to senior stakeholders we have not met. Use Nicholas's live 29 Jun thread to ask who signs off and request the intro — do not cold-outreach the EB in a champion-authored tender.

Close the competitive blind spot now: multiple bidders confirmed (29 Jun: 'reviewing all of our bidders proposals'), none named. Ask Nicholas who else is shortlisted so we differentiate on Merlin AI and the unified Maximo/OnBase flow, not price.

Differentiate on Merlin, not eInvoicing: eInvoicing is table-stakes across all bidders; lead the shortlist case with Merlin's AI negotiation agent, supplier-price-history intelligence and contract-anomaly detection to break a pure price tie ('pricing is in line with other vendors').

Lock commercial clarity: reconcile the $165,620 implementation fee against the $127,400 year-one SaaS fee (~$293k total) and schedule the PS scoping session so price ambiguity does not sink us in the comparison.

Quantify the business case with Finance/AP: eInvoicing interest is confirmed but ROI is qualitative. Partner with the AP/Finance owner (Robert Rummer) to model invoice volume, manual cost, error rate and target savings/cycle-time — a defendable ROI anchors the budget owner's approval.

Best Case forecast is not defensible: no budget-owner validation, no confirmed budget, single-threaded sponsorship, competitive process with unknown rivals. Honest category is Pipeline until the shortlist clears and the budget owner is engaged.

A defendable ROI case (e.g. '30% faster invoice processing, $X annual labor savings') strengthens our shortlist position vs price-only competitors and anchors the EB's budget approval.
